body_txt: In one of the Greek myths concerning the creation of humanity, the gods, angry with Prometheus and mankind, created a woman named Pandora to be a wife for Prometheus' brother Epimetheus, and gave her a jar that she was told to never open . However, the gods had also made her manipulative and cunning — later versions of the myth changed her motivation to innocent but dangerous curiosity — and she opened the box in secret. Within it were all the horrors and woes of life, which were now unleashed upon the earth to punish humanity forever after. In fiction, the Box most commonly appears closed, and must not be opened under any circumstances to avoid unleashing its evils into the world. Villainous characters may seek it out as a source of power, while heroes may seek to keep it out of their hands. If the Box was opened earlier or in the backstory, characters may instead try to force unleashed evil beings back into the thing. In the original story, it contained conceptual evils, so that opening it would cause people and the world to become more cruel, wicked, or selfish, alongside being stricken by evils such as age and disease. Some works keep this angle, while others go for a more visually dramatic version by having it be a prison for physical evils such as demons and monsters, who may or may not also cause or allegorically represent the ills of the original. An additional element is the Jar's final occupant — Elpis , Hope, the last being who did not leave the container when it was opened — whose nature and role can vary significantly between interpretations and works. In some cases, Hope staying behind is a single boon to mankind, as a comfort in the newly dark times; in others, Hope is simply one more torment amidst so many others, goading mankind onward with a promise of better times that will never come. In the original story, the container was identified as a jar — specifically a pithos , a large storage urn about as tall as a person — but was later changed to a box, as a result of the 16th-century translator Erasmus of Rotterdam mixing up pithos (πίθος) with pyxis (πυξίς), meaning "box". As a result, its modern appearance is a little inconsistant and can include a variety of boxes, jars, urns, and other containers. In more humorous works, the Box/Jar may be depicted as more eclectic containers such as lunchboxes, briefcases, or lockers. Sub-Trope of Artifact of Doom , Public Domain Artifact , and Sealed Evil in a Can .
